lines.content: > Use Junie to generate JUnit tests to ensure 100% coverage. Tests were verified manually to ensure correctness.
> Use Junie to generate JUnit tests to ensure close to 100% coverage, after project overhaul and significant feature
> Use Junie to generate JUnit tests to ensure close to 100% coverage, after a significant backend overhaul.
> Used claude code to create a template to work off of for the UI. Ended up scrapping the first version, and made a second version to fit the required functionalities.
> Used it for error testing and edge-case testing
> Took help when working on backend integration, especially when figuring out any missing backend integrations
> After creating a list of all backend functionalities such that they may be mapped to the GUI, used claude cowork to consolidate everything and plan the exact mapping
> Used claude for fixing the gui code when the backend mapping was not proper and there was a mix of logic and gui in the gui file

MealMeter is a restaurant feedback application that allows patrons to submit structured feedback about their dining experience directly at the point of sale. Patrons can rate food quality, cleanliness, and service, leave a written review, and optionally add tags. Owners can then review, sort, filter, organise, and manage the submitted feedback from a separate management view.
1. Ensure that Java `21` or above is installed on your computer.
2. Download the latest MealMeter release from the project's Releases page.
3. Copy the downloaded `.jar` file into the folder you want to use as MealMeter's home folder.
4. Open a terminal and `cd` into that folder.
For example, if the downloaded file is named `mealMeter-v1.jar`, run:
6. The MealMeter GUI should open in a few seconds.
- **Patron Feedback**: used by diners to submit reviews.
- **Owner Management**: used by the restaurant owner to manage submitted reviews.
Use the **Patron Feedback** tab to submit a new review.
1. Set a score for **Food Quality**, **Cleanliness**, and **Service**.
- Ratings can be adjusted in **0.5-point** steps.
2. Enter your comments in the **Your Review** text box.
3. Optionally add tags in the tags field.
- Added tags will be shown below the field.
4. Click **Submit Review** to save the review.
5. Click **Clear Form** if you want to reset all fields without submitting.
- A written review is required by the GUI before submission.
- Reviews are saved automatically after submission.
3. Enter the owner password and click **OK**.
If the password is correct, the Owner Management view will open. If it is incorrect, access will be denied and the application will return to the Patron Feedback tab.
Once logged in, the **Owner Management** tab shows a table of submitted reviews. The table includes:
From this screen, the owner can sort reviews, apply filters, mark reviews as resolved or outstanding, add or delete tags, delete reviews, refresh the table, and log out.
1. In the **Sort By** dropdown, choose one of the available criteria:
The table will refresh to show the sorted review list.
You can filter reviews using one or more of the following fields:
- **Include Tags**: only show reviews that contain all listed tags.
- **Exclude Tags**: hide reviews that contain any listed tags.
Tags should be separated by commas, for example:
Partial matching is also available for criteria such as:
You may enter multiple conditions separated by commas, for example:
After entering your filter settings, click **Apply Filter**.
- Leave fields blank if you do not want to use them.
- Use **Refresh** to return to the full review list after filtering or sorting.
- Condition input must match the implemented parser format exactly.
### Marking a Review as Resolved or Outstanding
To update a review's follow-up status:
1. Select a review from the table.
2. Click **Resolved** to mark it as resolved.
3. Click **Outstanding** to mark it as unresolved again.
This is useful for tracking whether a review has already been followed up on by the restaurant.
### Adding Tags to an Existing Review
To add tags to a review that has already been submitted:
1. Select a review from the table.
3. Enter one or more tags separated by commas.
MealMeter will add only the new tags. Existing matching tags will not be duplicated.
### Deleting Tags from an Existing Review
To remove tags from a review:
1. Select a review from the table.
3. Enter one or more tags separated by commas.
Only matching tags will be removed. Non-existent tags will be ignored and reported back to the user.
1. Select a review from the table.
Deleted reviews are removed from storage and cannot be recovered through the application.
Click **Refresh** to reload the full review list into the owner table.
- you want to clear the current filtered or sorted view
- new reviews were submitted while you were logged in
- you want to return to the latest saved state in the main review list
Click **Logout** to end the owner session.
- the application returns to the Patron Feedback tab
- the owner-only table is cleared from view
- owner-only actions are no longer available until you log in again
MealMeter stores review data locally in:
This file is created automatically if it does not already exist. Reviews are saved automatically whenever:
- an owner resolves or unresolves a review
- an owner adds or deletes tags
This means your data will still be available the next time you open the application from the same folder.
They are saved locally in `data/reviews.txt` relative to the folder where you run the application.
### How do I return to the full list after filtering or sorting?
Click **Refresh** in the Owner Management view.
### What is the default owner password?
### Can patrons access the owner features?
No. Owner features require successful login through the Owner Management tab.
### Do I need to save changes manually?
No. MealMeter saves review changes automatically.
Hello! Welcome to the guide section for the MealMeter application.
Whether you are trying out the app for the first time or contributing to the project, the documents below will help you get started.
Head over to this [User Guide](user-guide.md) to learn how to use MealMeter to submit, view, and manage restaurant feedback.
## Want to contribute to the codebase?
Head over to this [Developer Guide](developer-guide.md) to learn more about the design, architecture, and implementation of the application.
This section covers the technical documentation of MealMeter. The following documents cover the software design and
- Dependencies for the project can be found in the `build.gradle` file.
- Java JDK version 21 was used for development.
- Gradle version 8.5 was used for development.
MealMeter is a restaurant feedback application that allows patrons to provide structured feedback about their dining
experience directly at the point of sale.
The application enables customers to rate key aspects of their visit and leave written comments that help restaurants
understand customer satisfaction and identify areas for improvement.
The system collects ratings in three primary categories:
In addition to ratings, patrons must submit a short written review describing their experience.
This information is recorded and made available to the restaurant for analysis and service improvement.

Restaurants rely heavily on customer satisfaction to maintain their reputation and attract repeat customers. However, many establishments struggle to collect structured feedback from patrons during or immediately after their dining experience.
Current feedback mechanisms such as online review platforms often suffer from several limitations:
- Feedback is delayed or forgotten after customers leave the restaurant
- Reviews are often unstructured and challenging to analyse
- Restaurants may not receive feedback until long after the dining experience
- Restaurants may not be able to distinguish genuine reviews from bad-faith actors leaving review bombs
This project aims to provide restaurants with a simple and immediate way to collect meaningful feedback from patrons directly at the point of sale.
| As a... | I want to... | So that I can... |
| Restaurant Patron | leave a restaurant rating | express satisfaction or dissatisfaction with my dining experience |
| Restaurant Patron | leave a restaurant review | provide targeted feedback on what I think should be improved |
| Restaurant Patron | submit feedback quickly | leave the restaurant relatively quickly after making payment |
| Restaurant Owner | tag restaurant reviews based on the written review | categorise feedback based on each area of improvement |
| Restaurant Owner | sort reviews based on rating | evaluate positive and negative feedback separately |
| Restaurant Owner | filter reviews based on criteria such as rating or tags | view targeted feedback in categories such as food, service, and cleanliness |
| Restaurant Owner | mark reviews as resolved or outstanding | indicate which complaints have been addressed while maintaining an archive of all reviews |
| Restaurant Owner | delete reviews that are useless | focus on actual reviews from patrons |
- Restaurant Patrons should be able to give specific ratings for each service category
- Restaurant Patrons should be allowed to leave a written review
- Restaurant Owners should be able to tag reviews with custom tags for filtering
- Restaurant Owners should be able to view all patron reviews
- Restaurant Owners should be able to filter reviews by specified criteria
- Restaurant Owners should be able to sort reviews by specified criteria
- Restaurant Owners should be able to mark reviews as resolved or outstanding
- Restaurant Owners should be able to delete reviews
- The system should be able to store at least 100 user reviews
- The system should be able to sort and filter reviews within 2 seconds
- The interface should be simple and easy to use
- The system should not lose more than 5 minutes of work in the event of an application crash
- The application should not have a server component
MealMeter is a desktop-based restaurant feedback system intended for deployment at restaurant point-of-sale terminals or customer kiosks.
The system allows patrons to provide feedback through a simple interface consisting of:
Submitted feedback is processed by the application and stored locally for later viewing, sorting, filtering, and management by restaurant staff.
A `Main` class is the main entry point of the application, which initialises the UI and controller.
The system follows a 4-layer architecture structure consisting of:
- `Model`: Holds the data of the application in memory
- `Storage`: Reads data from, and writes data to, the hard disk
- Displays the Patron Feedback Panel to submit reviews
- Displays the Owner Management Panel to manage reviews
- Allows users to interact with the model via GUI elements, with the Controller acting as the intermediary
- Shows system messages and feedback after an action is completed
- Parses user input and converts it into command objects
- Executes user actions as commands on the model
- Handles sorting and filtering of reviews
- Manages review objects and review collections
- Coordinates saving and loading through the storage layer
- Loads reviews from a text file when the application starts
- Saves reviews to a text file when the review list is updated
- Handles file creation if the data file does not already exist
The above depicts the class diagram of the `Ui` component. This component is responsible for displaying the GUI of the
application. It is made up of the `MealMeterGui` class, which contains the two screens (`PatronPanel` and `OwnerPanel`)
The `MealMeterGui` class communicates with the `Model` component via the `Logic` component.
The above depicts a (partial) class diagram of the `Logic` component. Not depicted are the `Command` subclasses and
their individual dependencies to the enums and the model components.
As seen in the diagram, the main activity of this component is to intepret user input from the `Ui` and execute the
corresponding command on the `Model`. This is done so via `MealMeterController`, which creates the appropriate `Command`
(e.g. `AddReviewCommand`) object and executes it, returing a `CommandResult` object to the `Ui` to display to the user.
The `AuthManager` class is responsible for managing the authentication state of the application. Based on administrator
requirements, `Command` objects may require authentication to be executed.
The `Condition` subclasses are used by `FilterReviewsCommand`, while the `Criterion` and `SortOrder` enumerations are
Any `Command` that changes the `Model` will invoke `Storage` to save the updated model to the data file.
The above depicts the class diagram of the `Model` component. This component stores the `Review` information of the
application in memory. The `Model` offers wrapper methods for the `Logic` component to access and manipulate the data.
The `Model` loaded into memory is done by the `Storage` component on application initialisation and is saved by the
`Command` objects whenever a change is made to the data via the `Storage` component.
The above depicts the class diagram of the `Storage` component. This component is responsible for loading and saving
`Review` objects to the data file. During initialisation, the `Storage` component will attempt to load reviews from
A `StorageLoadResult` object is returned to the `Logic` component to
indicate if any errors occurred during loading or if the data file was successfully loaded, as well as the loaded

The above depicts the use case diagram of the application. The application is designed to be used by both restaurant
patrons and restaurant owners. However, most of the features are only available to restaurant owners, with patrons only
being able to leave a review. To access the owner features, the user must log in.
Additionally, as stated in the [Logic class diagram section](#logic-class-diagram), only actions that modify the model
will invoke the storage layer to save the updated model to the data file.

The `MealMeterGui` class handles interaction with the user. It is responsible for collecting user input and displaying
The `MealMeterController` class is responsible for executing user actions. The controller passes user input from
`MealMeterGui` to the respective `Command` objects and returns a `CommandResult` to `MealMeterGui` after the action is
The `Command` class represents an executable user instruction. Each command stores the information needed for execution.
The `Review` class represents a single customer review.
The `ReviewList` class stores and manages multiple `Review` objects.
The `Rating` class stores structured rating information for a review.
It also supports computing an overall rating value.
The `Tag` class represents a label attached to a review for categorisation and filtering.
The `Storage` class is responsible for persistent file handling.
- Create the storage file if necessary
The system uses structured rating categories:
This allows restaurants to analyse feedback more effectively than relying only on free-text reviews.
Feedback is collected directly at the point of sale. This improves response rates and helps restaurants capture
impressions while the dining experience is still fresh in the patron’s mind.
A 4-tier architecture was chosen to separate presentation, logic, and storage concerns. This improves modularity and
makes future enhancements easier to implement.
The system uses local file-based storage instead of a server-based backend. This keeps deployment simple and aligns
with the intended lightweight desktop application design.
This file documents the important APIs exposed by `MealMeterController` to `MealMeterGui`.
Submits a user review based on the user input from `MealMeterGui` in the `Patron Feedback` tab.

| `foodScore`        | `Double` | User input from the `Food Quality` spinner, clamped to a range of 1.0 to 5.0                                         |
| `cleanlinessScore` | `Double` | User input from the `Cleanliness` spinner, clamped to a range of 1.0 to 5.0                                          |

| `tagsAsString`     | `String` | Comma-separated user input from the `Tags (Optional)` text field, specifying the `Tag`s to include with the `Review` |
| `CommandResult` | A `CommandResult` object containing information after submitting the review |
Filters the reviews in the displayed `ReviewList` based on the user criteria specified in the `Owner Management` tab.

| `conditions`  | `String` | Comma-separated user input from the `Conditions:` text field, specifying the `Condition`s to filter by           |
| `CommandResult` | A `CommandResult` object containing information after filtering reviews |
Sorts the reviews in the displayed `ReviewList` based on specified user criteria in the `Owner Management` tab.

| `sortOrder` | `String` | User input from the `Order:` dropdown menu, specifying the `SortOrder` to sort by as a `String`   |
| `CommandResult` | A `CommandResult` object containing information after sorting reviews |
Resolves or unresolves the selected review in the displayed `ReviewList` in the `Owner Management` tab. These methods
will resolve the indexing differences between the saved and displayed `ReviewList`.
The methods are executed on the selected `Review`  in the displayed `ReviewList` when the user presses the `Resolved` or
| `rowIndex`         | `int`        | The index of the `Review` in the displayed `ReviewList` |
| `CommandResult` | A `CommandResult` object containing information after resolving or unresolving the `Review` |
Adds or deletes new `Tag`s from the selected review in the displayed `ReviewList` in the `Owner Management` tab. These

The methods are executed on the selected `Review` in the displayed `ReviewList` when the user presses the `Add Tags` or

| `CommandResult` | A `CommandResult` object containing information after logging in |
Logs the user out of the `Owner Management` tab when the `Logout` button is pressed. This revokes the user's access to
| `CommandResult` | A `CommandResult` object containing information after logging out |
This section describes the API of the `Storage` component.

The `Storage` component is responsible for persisting review data to disk and loading saved data back into the application at startup. It serves as the bridge between the in-memory model (`ReviewList`) and the text file used for long-term storage.
* loading reviews from a storage file,
* saving reviews to a storage file,
* returning non-fatal warnings when malformed stored data is encountered.

During loading, the storage component performs **best-effort parsing**. This means that if some review blocks in the file are malformed, those invalid blocks may be skipped while valid review blocks are still loaded successfully.
Constructs a `Storage` object that uses the default storage file path, `data/reviews.txt`.
| `Storage` | A `Storage` object using the default storage location. |
Constructs a `Storage` object with a custom file path.
This is useful when the application needs to use a non-default storage location, such as during testing.
| `storageFilePath` | `Path` | The path of the storage file to use. |
| `Storage` | A `Storage` object using the specified file path. |
Loads reviews from the storage file and returns them as a `ReviewList`.
If the storage file or its parent directories do not yet exist, they are created automatically.
This method is suitable when only the loaded review data is needed.
| `ReviewList` | The list of reviews loaded from the storage file. |
| `IOException` | Thrown when an error occurs during file creation or reading. |
Loads reviews from the storage file and returns both the loaded reviews and any non-fatal warnings encountered during loading.
* valid review blocks are loaded normally,
* malformed review blocks may be skipped,
* warnings are collected and returned instead of immediately failing the entire load.
This method is used when the caller needs both the loaded data and information about partial parsing issues.
| `StorageLoadResult` | Contains the loaded `ReviewList` and a list of non-fatal warnings. |
| `IOException` | Thrown when an error occurs during file creation or reading. |
Saves all reviews in the given `ReviewList` to the storage file.
The save operation rewrites the full contents of the storage file so that the file reflects the current in-memory review list.
| `reviewList` | `ReviewList` | The review list to be saved. |
| `IOException` | Thrown when an error occurs during file writing. |
| `ReviewList` | The review list loaded from the storage file. |
Returns the list of non-fatal warnings encountered while loading from storage.
These warnings typically indicate that some parts of the storage file were malformed, but that loading was still able to proceed for valid review entries.
| `List<String>` | The list of non-fatal loading warnings. |
* The storage component stores reviews in a text file.
* The full `ReviewList` is written back to disk whenever `saveReviews(...)` is called.
* The storage component is designed to be tolerant of malformed saved data by using warning-based best-effort loading.
* Fatal file operation failures are surfaced through `IOException`, while non-fatal parsing issues are returned as warnings in `StorageLoadResult`.
This document supplements the [developer guide](developer-guide.md) and provides information on the testing process.
1. Right-click the `test` folder and select `Run Tests in...`
2. Run `./gradlew clean test` in the Gradle console
Unit tests were completed for most of the classes in `Storage`, `Model` and `Logic` via Junit tests. 100% method
coverage was achieved, and close to 100% line coverage was achieved. This ensures that the backend functionality is as
Manual testing was done to ensure that the UI behaves as expected.

MealMeter is a restaurant feedback application that allows patrons to submit structured feedback about their dining experience directly at the point of sale. Patrons can rate food quality, cleanliness, and service, leave a written review, and optionally add tags. Owners can then review, sort, filter, organise, and manage the submitted feedback from a separate management view.
1. Ensure that Java `21` or above is installed on your computer.
2. Download the latest MealMeter release from the project's Releases page.
3. Copy the downloaded `.jar` file into the folder you want to use as MealMeter's home folder.
4. Open a terminal and `cd` into that folder.
For example, if the downloaded file is named `mealMeter-v1.jar`, run:
6. The MealMeter GUI should open in a few seconds.
- **Patron Feedback**: used by diners to submit reviews.
- **Owner Management**: used by the restaurant owner to manage submitted reviews.
Use the **Patron Feedback** tab to submit a new review.
1. Set a score for **Food Quality**, **Cleanliness**, and **Service**.
- Ratings can be adjusted in **0.5-point** steps.
2. Enter your comments in the **Your Review** text box.
3. Optionally add tags in the tags field.
- Added tags will be shown below the field.
4. Click **Submit Review** to save the review.

* Manages owner authentication for the application.
* This class is responsible for verifying the owner password and tracking
* whether owner-only access has been granted for the current application session.
* Constructs an {@code AuthManager} with the specified owner password.
* @param ownerPassword the password required for owner access
* @throws IllegalArgumentException if the password is null or blank
throw new IllegalArgumentException("Owner password cannot be null or blank.");
* Attempts to authenticate the owner using the provided password.
* If the password matches the stored owner password, owner access is granted.
* @param password the password entered by the user
* @return {@code true} if authentication succeeds, {@code false} otherwise
* Returns whether the owner is currently authenticated.
* @return {@code true} if owner access is currently granted, {@code false} otherwise
* Logs the owner out of the current session.
* Class representing a command to add a review.
* @param reviewBody the body of the review
* @param tagsToAddAsString the set of tags to add to the review
* Executes the command to add a review to the list.
* @return a {@code CommandResult} object containing the result of the command execution
* @throws InvalidArgumentException if any argument is in the wrong format
String.format("Food Score must be numbers between %.1f and %.1f.",
String.format("Cleanliness Score must be numbers between %.1f and %.1f.",
String.format("Service Score must be numbers between %.1f and %.1f.",
Rating rating = new Rating(foodScore, cleanlinessScore, serviceScore);
Review review = new Review(reviewBody, rating, tagsToAdd);
* Class representing a command to add tags to a review.
* @param index the index of the review to add tags to
* @param tagsToAddAsString the set of tags to add to the review
* Executes the command to add tags to a review.
* Tags that already exist in the review are not added again.
* @return a {@code CommandResult} object containing the result of the command execution
* @throws InvalidArgumentException if any argument is in the wrong format
* Class representing a generic user command.
* Returns true if the command should end the main program, else false.
* @return boolean representing if the command should terminate the main program
* Returns whether this command requires owner authentication.
